yaml.title: Problem Set 3 - Solutions
yaml.subtitle:
Economics 100B - Intermediate Microeconomic Theory
Prof. Muehlegger
yaml.author: Prof. Muehlegger

markdown.markdown:
Question 1: Monopsony
There are many small strawberry growers in the Central Valley. Let Q denote the number of tons of
strawberries that growers produce and P denote the price of strawberries.

The inverse supply curve for strawberries is given by:   P = (1/10)*Q
The inverse demand curve for strawberries is given by:   P = 120 – (1/10)*Q

a)       If there are many small buyers for strawberries, what will be market price and how many
tons of strawberries will be produced?

    If there are many small buyers, the market will be perfectly competitive. The competive equilibrium
    equates inverse supply and inverse demand.

    (1/10)*Q = 120 – (1/10)*Q -> Q = 600 -> P = 60.

b)     Now, suppose there is only a single buyer of strawberries. Write the expressions for
the monopsonist’s total expenditure and marginal expenditures on strawberries.

    The expression for total expenditure is TE = P*Q, where Q is given by the inverse supply
    curve.

    TE = (1/10)*Q * Q = (1/10)*Q2

    ME = dTE/dQ = (2/10)*Q

c)     With a single buyer, what will be market price and how many tons of strawberries will be
produced?

    The monopsonist (single buyer) will maximize profits by equating ME and the inverse demand curve.

    (2/10)*Q = 120 – (1/10)*Q -> (3/10)*Q = 120 -> Q = 400, P = 40

    d)      Draw the graph illustrating the monopsony decision. Be sure to include the inverse demand
    and supply curves of labor, the marginal expenditure curve, and label the equilibrium price and
    quantity of strawberries.

         120                                               ME
P                                                                              S
         80          CS

         40
                     PS
                                                                          D
         0
                                     400                   Q
    e)        Calculate consumer surplus, producer surplus, and deadweight loss.

         Consumer (firm) surplus = 1/2 *400*40+40*400 = $24,000

         Producers (strawberry grower) surplus = 1/2 *40*400 = $8,000

         DWL = 1/2 * 40 * 200 = $4,000

    f)     Briefly explain how the government might use a price floor / minimum price for
    strawberries to increase welfare.

         If the government sets a price floor / minimum price at the competitive price of $60, the monopsonist
         will have the incentive to purchase the competitive quantity of Q = 600. In this case, there will be no
         deadweight loss.

Question 2: Static Game Theory

Suppose two players are playing a game, Even and Odd. Each player has a quarter and must secretly turn
the quarter to heads or tails. The players then reveal their choices simultaneously. If the quarters match
(both heads or both tails), then Even keeps both quarters and wins one from Odd (+25 for Even, -25 for
Odd). If the quarters do not match (one heads and one tails) Odd keeps both quarters, and wins one from
Even (-25 for Even, +25 for Odd).

(a) Please draw the payoff matrix for this game.

(b) Does Even have a dominant strategy? Why or why not?

No. If Odd plays Tails, Even’s best response it to play Tails If Odd plays Heads, Even’s best response if to
play Heads.

(c) Does Odd have a dominant strategy? Why or why not?

No. Odd doesn’t want the coins to match. So if Even play “Heads,” Odd should play “Tails” and vice-
versa.

(d) Is there a Nash Equilibrium of this game?

No. There is no NE of the game. This is an example of the “Sibling game” we discussed in class. Either
Even or Odd would always like to update his or her strategy for any outcome of the game.

Question 3: Static Game Theory

Imagine a game with a Professor and Students (who all act together as one player). The Professor is giving
a final exam and has to decide whether to make it easy or hard. Students have to decide whether to put low
effort, medium effort, high effort, or max effort into studying for the exam. Both players decide
simultaneously.

Payoffs are as follows:

(a) Does Professor have a dominant strategy? Why or why not?

No. The professor doesn’t have a dominant strategy. A Hard Exam is better if the students extert “Max
Effort” or “High Effort”, but offers a lower payoff if students exert “Medium Effort” or “Low Effort.”

(b) Do Students have a dominant strategy? Why or why not? Are any of the student strategies
dominated? Why or why not?

The students don’t have a dominant strategy. If the exam is hard, they receive highest payoff by exerting
“Max Effort” But if the exam is Easy, they receive highest payoff by exerting “Medium Effort.”

There is a dominated strategy. “Low effort” yields a lower payoff than “Medium Effort” for the student
regardless of whether the exam is Easy or Hard.

(c) What is / are the Nash Equilibrium of this game?

After we eliminate “Low Effort” as a dominated strategy, we can evaluate other outcomes as potential Nash
Equilibrium. Here there are two NE. The first is (Easy Exam, Medium Effort). The professor doesn’t
want to switch the exam to Hard as it lowers his or her payoff and the students don’t want to exert more
effort for the same reason. The second is (Hard Exam, Maximum Effort), using a similar rationale.

(d) If there are multiple Nash Equilibria, which one will be selected in the end? If there is no Nash
Equilibrium, how will the game end?

We don’t know. Both options are Nash Equilibria.
